title: Volume backup strategy for git repos (AWS Lightsail)
description: Persistent volume at /data/repos holds all bare git repos. If Railway recycles or loses the volume, all wiki content is gone (DB has metadata but git content is lost). Need automated backup — e.g. periodic tar to S3/R2, or git bundle push to object storage.

title: Redesign clone URL UI to match GitHub's pattern
description:
## Problem

The current clone UI is a plain "Copy clone URL" button in a dropdown. It copies a bare URL like `https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/rsi-wiki.git`. This is unfamiliar to users who expect GitHub's clone dialog pattern, and it doesn't communicate what you're actually getting.

## Design

Replace with a GitHub-style clone popover:

1. **Tabbed protocol selector**: HTTPS (default) | SSH (future) | CLI
2. **Pre-filled command** in a monospace input box:
   - HTTPS tab: `git clone https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/rsi-wiki.git`
   - CLI tab: `curl -L https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/rsi-wiki.zip -o rsi-wiki.zip`
3. **Copy button** (clipboard icon) next to the input
4. **Info box** below: subtle note explaining what you're cloning

## The Two-Repo Problem

WikiHub has two repos per wiki:
- `<slug>.git` — authoritative (owner-only read/write)
- `<slug>-public.git` — derived mirror (everyone else, read-only)

The git backend already handles this transparently — the same URL routes to different repos based on auth:
- Owner with API key → authoritative repo (can push)
- Anyone else → public mirror (read-only, no private pages)

**This is fine.** The URL is the same either way. But the UI should communicate:
- For non-owners: "This clones the public content. Private pages are excluded."
- For owners: "This clones your full wiki including private pages. Use your API key as password."

## Info Box Copy

Non-owner view:
> "Clones public pages only. Private pages are excluded from this view."

Owner view:
> "Use your API key as the password. This includes all pages."

## No push-back problem

Unlike GitHub forks, there's no expectation of pushing back to someone else's wiki via git. The clone is read-only for non-owners (the git backend rejects pushes from non-owners). If someone wants to contribute, they fork via the API/UI, which creates a separate wiki they own. So there's no confusion about "I cloned it but can't push" — it's the expected behavior and matches GitHub's model for repos you don't have write access to.
close_reason: Implemented GitHub-style clone command UI. Info box for public/private context deferred.

title: style sidebar scrollbars: thin, hover-only, no visual clutter
description: the right panel inner scrollbar (and potentially left sidebar scrollbar) look ugly when they appear. fix: use thin scrollbar styling (scrollbar-width: thin, webkit-scrollbar 4px), only visible on hover. both sidebars should use position:sticky and only scroll independently when their content overflows the viewport. the main page scroll should be the primary scroll axis. test on admitsphere/Common-App which has a long left sidebar.

title: auto-generated table of contents from headings
description: auto-generate a table of contents from ## headings in rendered pages. use markdown-it-py TOC plugin or custom heading extraction. bonus: per-section [edit] links like Wikipedia/Farzapedia.

title: MCP server: expose wiki content for AI agent queries
description: instead of RAG with embeddings, expose wiki content via MCP server (model context protocol). any Claude Code or AI agent can connect to a wikihub MCP server and read/search wiki pages natively. the LLM does its own reasoning — no need for us to run embeddings. inspired by Farzapedia's /wiki-query (which just reads files) and gbrain's MCP layer.

title: download button: clarify wiki-level vs page-level download
description: when viewing a single page (not wiki root), the download button offers 'Download ZIP' and 'Copy clone URL' but these download the ENTIRE wiki, not just the current page. this is confusing. fix: (1) make the dropdown text explicit — e.g. 'Download wiki as ZIP' and 'Clone entire wiki'. (2) add a 'Download this page (.md)' option that downloads just the current page's markdown file. the page-level download is just a link to the .md content-negotiated URL.

title: Sidebar: show ACL-granted private pages to authorized non-owners
RSS feed for wiki changes
description: Atom/RSS feed at /@username/wiki.atom showing recent changes. Enables watch-style workflows without polling. Each commit becomes a feed entry with title=commit message, content=diff summary, link=commit URL. Also consider a global /feed.atom for all public changes (explore-level).

title: Editor and new-page experience is broken — needs full QA pass
description:
The entire editing-a-page-manually and creating-a-new-page web experience is borked. This is P0 — the editor is a core user flow.

Needs investigation:
- Can you create a new page from the web UI? Does the form work?
- Can you edit an existing page? Does the editor load? Does save work?
- Is the Milkdown editor bundled and loading correctly?
- Are there JS errors in the console?
- Does the raw textarea fallback work if Milkdown isn't loaded?
- Does visibility setting work when creating/editing?
- Does the breadcrumb/navigation work after save?

This blocks dogfooding — if you can't manually edit pages, the site is API-only.

title: Improve empty profile page text and populate example accounts
description: The text 'This person has not published a personal wiki landing page yet' is unhelpful. Replace with something minimal or hide entirely. Also populate jacobcole and other accounts with real content so the site doesn't look empty.
close_reason: Fixed empty profile text - only shows to owner now. Populated jacobcole profile.

title: Return 404 instead of 403 for unauthorized private pages to prevent enumeration
File support roadmap: from image embeds to Google Drive mode
description:
Progressive file support beyond markdown-only browsing. The spec already covers storing binaries in git repos and rendering Obsidian ![[image.png]] embeds, but there's no serving route (images 404) and the sidebar/UI is markdown-only.

**Progressive stages (each unlocks the next):**

Stage 0 — Serve binaries from git repo (bug fix, not feature)
- Route to serve raw files from authoritative/public repo via git cat-file
- Content-type detection, cache headers
- Prerequisite for ![[image.png]] embeds to actually render

Stage 1 — Image attachments (core LLMWiki need)
- Upload images alongside markdown pages
- Inline image preview in editor
- Image gallery or attachment panel per page
- LLM wikis need diagrams, charts, screenshots — this is table stakes

Stage 2 — File browser
- Show all files in sidebar/tree, not just .md
- File type icons
- Non-markdown files visible but clearly secondary to wiki pages

Stage 3 — Non-markdown preview
- Inline PDF viewer, CSV table render, image lightbox
- Reference material browsable alongside wiki pages

Stage 4 — Google Drive mode (future, maybe separate product)
- Drag-drop any file type, bulk upload
- Full-text search across non-markdown files
- Folder-first browsing (vs page-first)
- This diverges from LLMWiki core mission — needs careful product thinking

**Key question:** How far past Stage 1 should we go before it stops being an LLMWiki and starts being a document hosting platform? The Karpathy pattern is markdown knowledge bases maintained by LLMs — images support that, but a Google Drive clone doesn't.

**ListHub contrast:** ListHub is deliberately text-only (flat markdown items, no binary support). WikiHub already went further architecturally (git repos can hold anything), but the serving/browsing layer is markdown-only. The gap is between what git stores and what the UI shows.

**Spec reference:** Binary storage is already specced (wikihub-spec-final-2026-04-08.md, Data Architecture section). This epic covers the serving, browsing, and UX layers on top.

title: move TOC and backlinks to right sidebar instead of inline
description: currently TOC is at the top and backlinks at the bottom of the article, pushing content down and leaving empty space on the right. consider a right sidebar layout: TOC + backlinks (and later, wikilink graph) in a sticky right column, with a draggable border between the content area and right sidebar. this is a layout-sensitive change — the current two-column layout (left sidebar nav + content) would become three-column (left nav + article + right panel). IMPORTANT: this needs extensive testing on the dev server (localhost:5100) before pushing to production. test at multiple viewport widths, test on pages with no TOC (< 3 headings), test with and without backlinks, test mobile collapse behavior. do NOT push to prod without agent-browser verification at each breakpoint.

title: Curator Agent: inline AI sidebar for wiki curation and navigation
description:
## Vision

An inline AI sidebar on every wiki page — inspired by Neal Stephenson's Librarian (Snow Crash) and the Curator (Ready Player One). A conversational agent that knows who you are, what page you're on, and can take actions on wikis you have access to.

### Simplest use case
You're browsing someone's idea list. You say "promote this to the featured ideas list." The agent reads the current page, writes to the featured wiki (which is public-edit), done.

### Key references
- **Snow Crash Librarian** — already in WikiHub philosophy docs
- **Ready Player One Curator** — guides users through a vast archive
- **Noos project** — Jacob's existing coding-agent-backed knowledge graph (uses Claude Code under the hood)

---

## Architecture: Coding Agent with Filesystem Access (Community Standard)

### Research finding (2026-04-10)

The LLM Wiki ecosystem overwhelmingly uses **coding agents with direct filesystem access**, not API-mediated tool use:

| Pattern | Examples | Share |
|---------|----------|-------|
| **Coding agent (filesystem)** | SamurAIGPT (1.5k★), kfchou, Astro-Han, MehmetGoekce | ~80% |
| **MCP server (API-mediated)** | lucasastorian, sage-wiki | ~15% |
| **Desktop app** | nashsu | ~5% |

Karpathy's gist is literally a CLAUDE.md template — the agent opens a directory, reads the schema, and reads/writes markdown files directly. No API, no database, no custom tools.

### Why this matters for WikiHub

WikiHub's storage is already git repos of markdown files. The Curator doesn't need a custom tool-use layer — it can work on the files directly:

1. **Clone wiki repo** to a temp working directory
2. **Run coding agent** (Claude API with bash+file tools, or Claude Code subprocess) pointed at that directory
3. **Agent operates on markdown files natively** — the exact pattern the community uses
4. **Push changes back** via git push or WikiHub API
5. **Post-receive hook** syncs changes to DB (already exists)

This is simpler AND more capable than building custom API tools, because:
- Agent can do anything with the files (not limited to predefined tools)
- Matches how the entire community already works with LLM wikis
- CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md schema file is the standard discovery mechanism
- WikiHub's per-wiki AGENTS.md already exists and serves this purpose

### Execution model: Claude API with code execution tools

Use the Claude API (not a Claude Code subprocess) with these tools:
- `bash` — run shell commands (scoped to the temp working dir)
- `read_file` — read files in the working dir
- `write_file` — write files in the working dir
- `edit_file` — patch files

This gives coding-agent capabilities without process management complexity. The server:
1. Creates a temp dir per session
2. Clones the relevant wiki repo(s) into it
3. Runs Claude API calls with file/bash tools scoped to that dir
4. On agent actions that modify files, commits and pushes back to WikiHub
5. Cleans up temp dir when session ends

### Sandboxing (MVP vs Production)

| Approach | Isolation | Complexity | When |
|----------|-----------|------------|------|
| **Temp dir + chdir** | Process-level | Low | MVP — sufficient for trusted users |
| **Unix user per session** | User-level | Medium | v1.1 — if multi-tenant |
| **Docker per session** | Container-level | High | v2 — if running untrusted code |

For MVP, a temp directory with the agent's bash tool restricted to that path is adequate. The agent can only read/write files it was given access to.

### Multi-wiki operations

The "promote to featured" use case requires access to TWO wikis (source + destination). The temp dir can contain multiple cloned repos:

```
/tmp/curator-session-abc/
  jacobcole/ideas/          ← cloned from /@jacobcole/ideas
  wikihub/wikihub/          ← cloned from /@wikihub/wikihub (public-edit)
```

The agent's CLAUDE.md tells it the layout and what it can do.

---

## Agent Context (injected as system prompt / CLAUDE.md)

```
You are the Curator, WikiHub's AI librarian.

## Current session
- User: @{username}
- Page: /@{owner}/{wiki}/{page}
- Permissions: {read/write/admin}

## Working directory layout
- {owner}/{wiki}/          ← the wiki you're viewing
- wikihub/wikihub/         ← the main WikiHub wiki (public-edit)
- {other cloned wikis as needed}

## How to make changes
Edit files directly. Changes are auto-committed and pushed to WikiHub.
Follow the wiki's schema.md for structure rules.

## Available wikis for this user
{list of wikis the user can read/write}
```

---

## MVP Scope

### Backend
- `POST /api/v1/agent/chat` — SSE endpoint, requires auth
  - Input: `{message, conversation_id?, page_context?}`
  - Output: SSE stream of agent text + tool call results
  - Creates temp dir, clones relevant repos, runs Claude API with file tools
- Session management: temp dir per conversation, cleanup on timeout
- Git integration: auto-commit + push on file writes

### Frontend
- Slide-out panel on right side of page (does not replace left nav sidebar)
- Toggle button in page actions area (or floating button)
- Chat input at bottom, messages scroll up
- Tool calls shown inline (collapsed by default, expandable)
- "Curator is editing..." indicator when writing
- Desktop only for MVP; hidden on mobile

### System prompt
- Injected with user identity, current page, wiki structure
- References the wiki's own AGENTS.md/schema.md for rules
- Tells agent to work directly with files in the temp dir

---

## Chrome Extension (v2)

A personal Curator that works on any website:
- Sidebar Chrome extension (connects to Cortex project)
- On WikiHub pages: uses the built-in Curator API
- On other sites: personal agent mode (save to your wiki, annotate)
- If built-in sidebar AND extension both present:
  - Extension detects WikiHub, defers to built-in (or takes over — user pref)

---

## Immediate Next Steps
1. Make wikihub/wikihub wiki public-edit
2. Add /api/v1/agent/chat SSE endpoint
3. Implement temp-dir clone + file tools for Claude API
4. Build frontend slide-out panel
5. Test: "promote this idea to featured" end-to-end

## Files involved
- NEW: app/routes/agent_chat.py — SSE chat endpoint + session management
- NEW: app/agent_tools.py — bash/read/write/edit tool definitions for Claude API
- NEW: app/templates/_curator_panel.html — sidebar UI
- MODIFY: app/templates/reader.html — add Curator toggle + panel
- MODIFY: app/__init__.py — register blueprint
- MODIFY: requirements.txt — add anthropic SDK

## Dependencies
- Anthropic API key in server .env
- Claude API with tool use (streaming)
- Git installed on server (already is)
close_reason: MVP shipped: SSE endpoint at /api/v1/agent/chat with Claude-powered file tools, slide-out panel UI in reader.html, anthropic SDK added to requirements

title: right panel scrollbar positioned too far left — should hug right edge
description: the thin-scroll scrollbar on the right sidebar panel appears too far to the left instead of sticking to the right edge of the panel. likely needs padding adjustment or the scrollbar track needs to be right-aligned. visible on pages with long TOC like admitsphere/Common-App.

title: Cross-wiki wikilinks: [[/@user/wiki/page]] syntax
description: Platform-layer cross-wiki linking. Any wikilink starting with /@ resolves across wikis. No git submodules. Resolver checks target page existence across wiki_ids. Unresolved cross-wiki links render red-dashed. Documented in spec 2026-04-10.

title: Auto-convert uploads to Karpathy LLM Wiki format
description:
## Context

When someone uploads a zip of markdown files (e.g., an Obsidian vault, a folder of dated notes, Jeremy Nixon's thinking index), can we auto-detect structure and post-process it into Karpathy's LLM Wiki format?

## Karpathy format target
- `raw/` — immutable source docs
- `wiki/` — LLM-generated/maintained pages with frontmatter (title, tags, status, confidence, contradictions, open_questions)
- `index.md` — content catalog with one-line summaries per page
- `log.md` — append-only activity log
- `schema.md` / `CLAUDE.md` — conventions and domain rules
- Cross-references: symmetric wikilinks, key claims, connections sections

## What we'd need to detect
- Date patterns in filenames or content (daily notes → log.md entries)
- Existing wikilinks `[[...]]` (Obsidian native)
- Folder structure heuristics (e.g., `daily/`, `templates/`, `attachments/`)
- Frontmatter already present vs needs generating
- Which files are "source material" vs "synthesis/wiki pages"

## Open questions
- **Option vs default?** Should this be opt-in ("Convert to LLM Wiki format") or automatic on upload?
- **Destructive or additive?** Do we reorganize their files into raw/wiki/, or just add the missing pieces (index.md, log.md, frontmatter) on top of existing structure?
- **LLM-powered or rule-based?** Simple heuristics might handle 80% (date parsing, wikilink extraction, folder detection). Full Karpathy format (key claims, contradictions, confidence scores) probably needs an LLM pass.
- **When does it run?** On zip upload? On git push? As an async "maintain" action?
- **How does this relate to WikiHub's built-in maintenance vision?** (see llm-wiki-ecosystem wiki — WikiHub Opportunity page)

## Possible approach
1. Rule-based first pass: detect dates, extract wikilinks, generate index.md, infer frontmatter from filenames
2. Optional LLM pass: classify pages as entity/concept/source, generate key claims, find contradictions
3. Surface as "Organize" button on wiki page, or auto-suggest after zip upload

title: Signup form not detected by password managers (1Password)
description:
## Problem

The signup / create account page is not recognized by 1Password (and likely other password managers) as an account creation form. 1Password does not offer to autofill or save credentials.

## Likely cause

Password managers detect signup forms via:
- `autocomplete="new-password"` on the password field
- `autocomplete="username"` or `autocomplete="email"` on the identity field
- `<form>` element wrapping the inputs
- Input `name` and `type` attributes (`type="email"`, `type="password"`, `name="email"`)

If any of these are missing or non-standard (e.g., using `<div>` instead of `<form>`, custom input components without proper attributes, or missing `autocomplete` hints), password managers won't detect it.

## Fix

Ensure the signup form has:
1. A `<form>` element wrapping all inputs
2. `<input type="email" name="email" autocomplete="email">` for the email field
3. `<input type="password" name="password" autocomplete="new-password">` for the password field
4. `<input type="text" name="username" autocomplete="username">` for the username/display name field
5. A `<button type="submit">` (not just an onclick handler)

Test with 1Password browser extension after fixing — it should offer to save new credentials on submit.

title: Explore cards: hide empty stats instead of placeholder text
close_reason: Implemented in this session

title: Serve binary files from git repo (images 404)
description:
![[image.png]] Obsidian embeds are supported in the renderer but there's no route to actually serve binary files from the git repo. Images referenced in markdown pages 404.

Needs a route that serves raw files from the authoritative or public mirror repo via git cat-file, with proper content-type detection and cache headers. Should respect the same auth dispatch as other wiki reads (owner gets authoritative repo, everyone else gets public mirror).

This is Stage 0 of the file support roadmap (wikihub-291) — prerequisite for any image/attachment functionality to work.

title: Split folder into standalone wiki
description: Promote a folder from personal wiki into its own project wiki. git subtree split extracts history, creates Wiki row, reindexes pages. Optionally replaces folder with cross-wiki link. One-way promotion only. v2 feature.

title: Add Karpathy LLM Wiki format guide to wikihub/wikihub docs
description:
Add a page explaining the Karpathy LLM Wiki format to the official wikihub/wikihub wiki. This should help new users understand the format WikiHub is built to host.

Key content: directory structure (raw/wiki/schema), frontmatter fields, the three operations (ingest/query/lint), page content structure (summary, key claims, connections, contradictions), index.md, log.md, CLAUDE.md roles.

Think carefully about where this fits in the wiki's organization and the new user experience. Consider the existing pages: Getting Started, Agent Integration, API Reference, Examples, philosophy/*.
close_reason: Page created and index updated

title: Explore: rename 'Recent' to 'Wikis' and list all wikis
description: The bottom section of /explore currently says 'Recent' and only shows a limited set. It should say 'Wikis' and comprehensively list all public wikis on the platform. At this stage we have few enough wikis that showing all of them is better than an arbitrary cutoff. Can add pagination later when there are hundreds.
close_reason: Implemented: Explore now shows Editorial Picks → all Wikis → People

title: Rename visibility levels for clarity: public→public-view, unlisted→unlisted-view
description:
## Problem

The current visibility names are confusing. 'public' doesn't make it clear that it means read-only (no editing). Users could reasonably think 'public' means anyone can edit.

## Proposed Naming

| Current | New | Meaning |
|---------|-----|---------|
| public | public-view | Anyone can read, only owner can edit |
| public-edit | public-edit | Anyone can read AND edit |
| unlisted | unlisted-view | Only people with the link can read |
| private | private | Only owner/ACL can read |

## Scope

- Update the `VALID_VISIBILITIES` set in `app/acl.py`
- Update all references in routes, templates, models
- Update frontmatter parsing to accept both old and new names (backwards compat)
- Update the visibility badge labels in reader.html, folder.html
- Update API docs
- Migration: existing pages with 'public' should map to 'public-view', 'unlisted' to 'unlisted-view'
- Keep accepting old names in frontmatter for backwards compatibility (Postel's Law)

## Files involved
- `app/acl.py` — VALID_VISIBILITIES
- `app/routes/api_wikis.py` — visibility endpoints
- `app/templates/reader.html` — visibility badges
- `app/templates/folder.html` — visibility badges
- `app/models.py` — any visibility defaults
- `wikihub-spec-final-2026-04-08.md` — update spec

## Notes
Candidate for Codex — thorough rename across codebase.

title: Profile sidebar: show all wikis for any user
close_reason: Implemented in this session

title: Review: Editorial Picks on Explore — how should curation work?
description:
Currently Editorial Picks on /explore is hardcoded to show @wikihub/wiki as a single wiki card. This was auto-generated code, not a deliberate product decision.

Options:
1. **Current: hardcoded @wikihub/wiki card** — just shows the official wiki. Not really 'editorial picks.'
2. **Pages from @wikihub/wiki as picks** — the Featured Wikis page we created has curated links. Could render those as cards instead of showing the wiki container.
3. **Tag/flag system** — wikis get an 'editorial_pick' flag, admins toggle it. Explore queries for flagged wikis.
4. **Curated from the community index wiki** — @wikihub/wikihub Featured Wikis page is the source of truth. A special tag or frontmatter field marks wikis for the explore page.
5. **Remove editorial section entirely** — just show popular (by stars) and recent. Let the @wikihub wiki be the editorial venue, not the explore page.

The community index wiki (@wikihub/wikihub/Featured Wikis) already exists as editorial curation. The question is whether /explore should also have its own editorial section or just link to the community wiki.

title: Landing page: 'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity' CTA
description:
Consider adding secondary CTAs on the landing page hero alongside 'Publish a wiki in seconds'. Candidates:

1. **'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity'** — the grand vision, taps into Snow Crash / Librarian philosophy. wikihub isn't just a tool, it's a collective knowledge project.

2. **'Make private collaborative lists with agents'** — the practical agent use case. Not everything is public publishing — private wikis where your agent writes and organizes alongside you.

3. **'Agent-native Google Drive'** — positions wikihub as the file system / knowledge layer that agents can actually read and write to, unlike Google Drive. Markdown files in git = structured, versionable, API-accessible. Google Drive is for humans with browsers; wikihub is for humans AND agents. This framing resonates with the 'why not just use Notion/Google Docs' objection — those tools have terrible agent APIs.

These could be secondary CTAs, tagline variants, rotating hero text, or value prop cards below the fold. The key insight: wikihub has three distinct value props for three audiences:
- **Publishers** → 'Publish a wiki in seconds' (Karpathy wave, Obsidian users)
- **Visionaries** → 'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity' (knowledge builders, researchers)
- **Agent users** → 'Agent-native Google Drive' or 'Collaborate with agents on private knowledge' (developers, AI power users)

title: hover dropdowns disappear when moving mouse to options
description: multiple dropdown menus across the site use hover to show/hide, but there's a gap or timing issue that causes the dropdown to disappear before the user can reach it. known instances: (1) download/clone dropdown on wiki pages — hover the download icon, try to move mouse down to the options, dropdown vanishes. (2) account menu in top-right nav — same issue. (3) hamburger menu on mobile. audit ALL hover-based dropdowns site-wide and fix them all. likely fix: add a small padding/bridge between trigger and dropdown, or switch to click-to-toggle with click-outside-to-close.

title: Visibility indicators: subtle lock/globe icons in sidebar and listings
description:
Make it obvious which pages are public vs private without being overwhelming. Options:
- Lock icon on private pages only (public is the default, no icon needed)
- Or globe icon on public pages only
- Show in sidebar items and content listings (folder view, explore cards)
- Should be very subtle — small, muted color, doesn't clutter the sidebar
- The current vis-badge on page view is fine; this is about the sidebar/listing context where you scan many items

title: infobox rendering from frontmatter metadata
description: render certain frontmatter keys as a Wikipedia-style infobox sidebar on pages. e.g. person pages get photo, 'known for', 'current role'. pure template work on top of existing frontmatter parsing. inspired by Farzapedia's person pages.

title: Webhook notifications for wiki changes
description: Allow users to register webhook URLs that receive POST notifications when pages in their wikis change. Payload: {event, wiki, page, author, sha, timestamp}. Enables Slack/Discord integrations, CI pipelines, downstream sync. API: POST /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/webhooks to register.

title: Google OAuth login not working despite correct provider console config
description: Google OAuth is configured in the Google Cloud Console with the correct redirect URIs, but login flow is failing. Needs collaborative debugging with Harrison to diagnose — could be callback URL mismatch, client secret issue, or Flask-side handling. Requires hands-on investigation together.

title: Per-user activity feeds
description: Each user gets an activity feed at /@username/activity showing their recent edits across all wikis. This is the agent-first equivalent of a GitHub contribution graph. Shows: page creates, edits, forks, stars. API: GET /api/v1/users/:username/activity. Web: rendered as timeline on profile or dedicated page.

title: Curator Agent: inline sidebar agent for wiki curation (Snow Crash / Ready Player One inspired)
description:
## Vision

An inline curator agent sidebar in the spirit of the Librarian from Snow Crash and the Curator from Ready Player One. An AI assistant that lives in the WikiHub UI sidebar and can take actions on your behalf — curate, cross-reference, promote content between wikis.

## Example Use Case

You're browsing someone's idea list. You want to promote an idea to the Featured Wikis page. The curator agent can:
1. Read the current page context (who you are, what page you're on)
2. Take actions with your permissions (read/write wikis you have access to)
3. Append content to public-edit pages, create pages in your own wikis, etc.

## Architecture Considerations

### Agent Backend
- **Claude Code under the hood** (like Noos project) — coding agent with file/API tools
- Should run in **print mode** for streaming responses to the sidebar
- Needs the current user's API key for WikiHub API calls
- Must be aware of: logged-in user, current page URL, current wiki context

### Security / Isolation
- Agent runs with the permissions of the current user (their API key)
- Must NOT get access to wrong directories on the server
- **MVP: Unix user permissions** may be sufficient — each agent session runs as a restricted user with only API access (no filesystem access beyond temp dirs)
- **Future: Docker containers** for full isolation if needed
- Agent should only interact via the WikiHub API, not touch git repos directly

### Parallel Agents
- Architecture should support multiple users running curator agents simultaneously
- Each agent session is stateless — gets user context at start, runs, returns result
- Server-side: queue or spawn model per request

### UI
- Sidebar panel on the right side of wiki pages
- Chat-like interface: user types a request, agent streams a response
- Agent can show proposed changes before applying them
- Minimizable/collapsible

### Chrome Extension (v2)
- Personal agent sidebar that works on any website
- If Chrome extension sidebar is open, WikiHub's built-in sidebar hides (avoid duplication)
- Extension would use the same WikiHub API key
- Relates to the Cortex project for personal agent infrastructure
- Could detect if user is logged into WikiHub and auto-configure

## MVP Scope (v1)
1. Right sidebar panel on wiki pages (behind feature flag or logged-in only)
2. Chat input → sends to Claude API with system prompt including page context
3. Agent can call WikiHub API endpoints (search, read_page, create_page, update_page, append_section)
4. Streams response back to sidebar
5. No filesystem access — API-only

## Open Questions
- Unix permissions vs Docker for agent isolation?
- Should the agent have its own API key or use the user's?
- Rate limiting per user?
- How to handle the Chrome extension ↔ built-in sidebar coordination?

title: Review: bio/profile frontmatter in personal wiki — Claude-proposed, needs human sign-off
description:
The personal wiki profile-aware frontmatter (bio:, avatar:, links: fields in index.md) was proposed by Claude and added to the spec at 2026-04-10 03:45 PT in commit 10633ac. It follows the GitHub profile README pattern — your personal wiki index.md doubles as your profile page with special frontmatter fields rendered in the profile header.

Open questions:
- Is frontmatter the right place for bio/avatar/links, or should these be DB fields editable via a settings UI?
- Should bio come from frontmatter at all, or should the rendered markdown body BE the profile (no special fields)?
- Is the GitHub profile README pattern the right model, or is something like a Notion-style profile page better?
- How does this interact with agents writing to the personal wiki — should an agent be able to update your bio?

Currently working fine as-is. This is a design review, not a bug.

title: bulk permission changes
description: no way to bulk change permissions on multiple pages at once. need ability to select multiple files and change visibility/ACL in one action.

title: draggable resize handle between content and right sidebar
description: add a draggable border/handle between the article content area and the right sidebar panel. user can drag to resize the split. store preference in localStorage. similar to VS Code's panel resize. lower priority than scrollbar fix.

title: NOTABLE.md note is wrong: API accounts CAN log in via web UI
description: NOTABLE.md says API-created accounts can't log in via web UI. This is incorrect — the login page has 'Sign in with API key' which works for API-only accounts. The note should be removed or corrected. Low priority — just docs cleanup.

title: Sidebar: collapsible folders that auto-expand to current page
description:
## Problem

When navigating directly to a deep page like /@wikihub/wikihub/philosophy/The Librarian, the sidebar shows the folder tree but:
1. Folders aren't collapsible — the full tree is always expanded or always collapsed
2. When landing on a nested page, the relevant folder isn't auto-opened to show where you are

## Expected behavior

- Sidebar folders should be collapsible (click to toggle open/closed)
- When navigating directly to a page, the folder containing that page should auto-expand
- Other folders should default to collapsed, keeping the sidebar clean
- The current page should be visually highlighted and scrolled into view

## Files involved

- `app/templates/reader.html` — sidebar rendering macro + JS for toggle
- `app/templates/folder.html` — same sidebar rendering
- `app/routes/wiki.py` — `_build_sidebar_tree()` already sets `active` flag on items

## Notes

The toggle JS (`toggleFolder()`) and CSS (`.sidebar-folder.open`) already exist. The issue is that the default open/closed state isn't intelligently set based on the current page path.
close_reason: Sidebar folders collapse by default, auto-expand to active page, scroll active item into view. Consistent JS across reader.html, folder.html, profile.html.

title: Private band UI warning
description: No visual confirmation that <!-- private --> bands are working. Users need to see their page has private content. Show warning in rendered page view.
close_reason: Added private-band warning surfaces plus AST-aware band stripping.

title: v1.5 milestone
description: Near-term features building on v1 foundation: wikilink graph, structured data, API safety, viral growth.

title: D3 force-graph of wikilinks
description: Wikilink model already tracks [[link]] refs between pages. Need: (1) API endpoint to dump graph as JSON (nodes + edges), (2) page with D3 force layout visualization. ~80 lines of D3. High demo value.

title: JSON-LD structured data on pages
description: Add <script type=application/ld+json> block to page template with @type: Article, title, author, dateModified. Trivial effort, free SEO/discoverability.

title: Optimistic locking (If-Match / ETag / 409)
description: Pages have git commit SHAs. Return SHA as ETag on GET, require If-Match on PUT, 409 if stale. Prevents silent data loss from concurrent edits.

title: Twitter auth / tweet-to-verify signup
description: Signup requires tweeting a specific post and pasting tweet URL to verify. Viral distribution + lightweight sybil resistance. Agent-compatible via Twitter API key.

title: Merge People into Explore (single page, people + wikis)
close_reason: Implemented in this session

title: Standardize account nav dropdown and enrich settings page
description: Replace the current top-bar Settings/Logout links with a standard account menu attached to the user avatar/name, and make the settings page feel like a complete destination instead of a blank utility page.
acceptance_criteria: Authenticated header shows an account trigger that exposes Settings and Logout in a menu; menu works on hover and keyboard/focus. Settings page includes clearer sections and context for account, profile entry points, and API keys while keeping existing functionality.
close_reason: Implemented account dropdown navigation, expanded settings page, updated spec, and verified with full e2e suite.

title: Per-wiki llms.txt endpoint
description: /@user/wiki/llms.txt — site-wide exists but per-wiki version is what agents want when working with a specific wiki. Trivial to add.
close_reason: Added per-wiki /@user/wiki/llms.txt and /llms-full.txt endpoints.

title: bulk delete and folder delete
description: cannot delete an entire wiki. cannot bulk delete files. cannot delete a folder. no delete button visible in the file list when browsing a folder. need: delete wiki, delete folder, bulk file delete, delete buttons in list view.

title: Mobile: sidebar toggle + basic mobile UX audit
description:
## Problem

The left sidebar is not accessible on mobile. There's no hamburger menu or toggle button to open it. This means mobile users can't navigate between pages within a wiki — they're stuck on whatever page they landed on.

## Scope

1. **Sidebar toggle on mobile** — add a hamburger/menu button that opens the sidebar as an overlay on small screens
2. **Basic mobile UX audit** — verify all core flows work on mobile:
   - Reading pages (text wrapping, tables, code blocks)
   - Navigation (breadcrumbs, sidebar, wikilinks)
   - Profile page layout
   - Search
   - Login/signup forms
   - Creating/editing pages (if logged in)

## Files involved
- `app/templates/reader.html` — has sidebar-overlay infrastructure but may lack a toggle button
- `app/templates/folder.html` — same
- `app/templates/base.html` — global nav bar, may need hamburger
- CSS in each template's style block — media queries for mobile
close_reason: Hamburger menu added to reader, folder, and profile pages. Opens sidebar as fixed overlay on mobile.

title: Consistent nav across all pages (landing + inner)
close_reason: Implemented in this session

title: backlinks: surface 'what links here' from wikilinks table
description: surface 'what links here' backlinks in the page sidebar. data already exists in the wikilinks table — just need to query and render. like Wikipedia's 'what links here' feature.

title: Graph coloring: only color by folder when subfolders actually exist
description: When coloring graph nodes by folder, if the wiki has no subfolders (just files at the root level), every single node gets a different color because each file is treated as its own 'folder'. Should only apply folder-based coloring when there are actual subdirectories. If everything is flat at the root, use a single color for all nodes.

title: Design community index wiki (@wikihub as curated meta-index)
description:
The @wikihub/wikihub wiki should serve as a curated community index — an 'awesome list of awesome lists.' User stories:

1. A user creates a great 'Best Body Masters List' wiki. They want it featured.
2. The community wiki has a page like 'Best Fitness Wikis' that links to it with a blurb.
3. A visitor lands on @wikihub and can browse curated categories pointing to the best user wikis.

This is different from /explore (algorithmic, by stars/activity). The community wiki is editorial — someone decides what's featured.

Key questions to resolve:
- Is @wikihub/wikihub the right place for this, or should there be a separate @wikihub/featured wiki?
- How do categories work? Pages in the wiki like 'Best AI Tools Wikis', 'Best Reading Lists'?
- Should featured wikis get a badge/indicator on their own pages?
- Who can edit the community index? Just admins, or suggested-edit PRs from users?
- How does this interplay with the Explore page? Does Explore pull from the community wiki's editorial picks?

For v1: probably just manually curated pages in @wikihub/wikihub that link to user wikis with [[cross-wiki wikilinks]] or plain URLs. The index IS the wiki — no special machinery needed. Editorial curation is just writing markdown.

Reference: ListHub had similar brainstorming about community lists as indexes of other lists.

title: Revert to arbitrary history state
description: Add ability to revert a page or wiki to any previous commit. Agent-first: API endpoint POST /wikis/:owner/:slug/revert with {sha, path?}. Web UI: 'Revert to this version' button on the diff view page. Should create a new commit (not force-push), so the revert itself is tracked in history.

title: Sidebar: distinguish folders from cross-wiki links in navigation
description:
## Problem

When navigating the sidebar, there's no visual distinction between:
1. **Folders** — subfolders within the current wiki (e.g. `ideas/` folder inside `jacobcole/jacobcole`)
2. **Wiki links** — links that take you to a different wiki entirely (e.g. from personal wiki to `jacobcole/ideas`)

Both use the same folder/page icons and indentation. Users can't tell if clicking will keep them in the current wiki or jump to a different one.

Currently the sidebar only shows items from the current wiki's file tree. Cross-wiki links only appear in page content as regular markdown links. The profile page has a separate "Wikis" section, but this pattern isn't used elsewhere.

## Proposed Solutions (pick one or combine)

### Option A: Wiki icon in sidebar (minimal)
Add a third icon type — a small "wiki" icon (e.g. book/globe/grid) — distinct from the folder and page icons. When the sidebar lists sub-wikis (e.g. on a profile or index page), use this icon instead of folder.

### Option B: "Your wikis" sidebar section
Like the profile page already does — show a separate labeled section "WIKIS" below the file tree in the sidebar. This already works on profile.html (lines 176-185). Extend to reader.html and folder.html when viewing a user's personal wiki.

### Option C: Breadcrumb-aware context switching
When you click from one wiki into another, the breadcrumb should make it visually obvious you've changed context — e.g. the sidebar header changes from "JACOB'S IDEAS" to the new wiki's name, with a "← back to profile" link. (This already happens, but the transition is jarring with no warning.)

### Option D: Wiki cards in page content
When the index page links to other wikis, render those as visually distinct cards (title + description + page count) rather than plain text links. This makes it obvious in the content area that these are whole wikis, not pages.

## Recommendation
Start with **Option B** — it's the simplest and profile.html already has the pattern. Then add **Option A** (wiki icon) for polish. Option D is nice for landing pages but is a bigger change.

## Files involved
- `app/routes/wiki.py` — `_build_sidebar_tree()` builds the sidebar
- `app/templates/reader.html` — sidebar rendering macro (lines 181-209)
- `app/templates/folder.html` — sidebar rendering macro (lines 47-69)
- `app/templates/profile.html` — already has the "Wikis" section pattern (lines 176-185)
close_reason: Implemented Option B + A: sidebar now shows a 'Jacob Cole's Wikis' section with book icons below the current wiki's file tree. Applied to reader.html, folder.html, and profile.html. Deployed to production.

title: Graph system: page graph, full graph, filtered graph with progressive coloring
description:
## Context

Wikis are graphs. The wikilink structure already contains rich relationship data — we just need to visualize it. This spec defines a three-view graph system that works on any wiki (structure-agnostic) but looks better with metadata (progressive enhancement).

## Three views, one graph engine

Underlying data: nodes = pages, edges = wikilinks. Derived from the wikilinks index wikihub already builds for backlinks. No separate graph database needed.

### 1. Page graph (every page sidebar/footer)
- Current page = center node, highlighted
- Show all pages directly linked TO or FROM this page (1-hop)
- Optionally expand to 2-hop (friends of friends)
- Click node → navigate to that page
- Like Obsidian's local graph — scoped, useful, not overwhelming
- Must be fast enough to render inline on every page load

### 2. Full graph (wiki homepage + dedicated route)
- Every page is a node, every wikilink is an edge
- Force-directed layout — clusters emerge naturally from link density
- The "wow" view showing the shape of the whole knowledge base
- Zoom, pan, search, click-to-navigate
- Shown on wiki homepage (index.md) as an interactive embed and at a dedicated `/@user/wiki/graph` route

### 3. Filtered graph (overlays on full graph)
- Filter by: directory path, frontmatter `type:`, tag, or search query
- "Show only pages connected to X" — page graph launched from full graph
- "Hide all conversation pages" — reduce noise to see topic structure
- Toggle filters on/off, combine them

## Coloring strategy (progressive enhancement)

The graph looks good with zero schema knowledge and great with it:

| Signal | How to use | Agnostic? |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Directory path | `wiki/courses/` = blue, `wiki/topics/` = green, etc. | Yes — any wiki with folders |
| Frontmatter `type:` | Override directory color if present, more precise | Semi — only typed wikis |
| Frontmatter `tags:` | Secondary color ring or icon badge | Semi |
| Link count | Node size scales with inbound + outbound links | Yes — derived from wikilinks |
| Recency | Opacity or glow based on `updated:` or git date | Semi |

**Fallback:** no directories, no frontmatter, no nothing → every node same color, sized by link count. Still works. Degrades gracefully.

**Key rule:** never require metadata for the graph to function. Only use it to enhance when present.

## Where graphs appear

- **Page sidebar/footer**: page graph (1-hop), always visible, lightweight
- **Wiki homepage (index.md)**: full graph embedded inline — interactive `<canvas>` or collapsible section. WikiHub auto-injects on index.md pages, or expose a `{{graph}}` embed directive
- **Dedicated `/graph` route**: full-screen interactive graph with filter controls, per wiki

The homepage embed doesn't violate structure-agnosticism — it's a rendering feature of the homepage route, not imposed on content.

## Tech notes

- **Data source:** wikilinks index (already exists for backlinks). Endpoints: `GET /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/graph` returning `{nodes: [{id, title, type?, dir?}], edges: [{source, target}]}`
- **Frontend:** d3-force or similar force-directed layout. Lightweight, browser-native.
- **Performance:** wikis under 500 pages — render all nodes. Above that — progressive loading or cluster-first-then-expand. Page graph (1-hop) must be instant.
- **Interaction:** hover = show title + link count, click = navigate, drag = reposition, scroll = zoom

title: wiki upload from drag and drop is broken
description: drag and drop wiki upload doesn't work. needs investigation — check the upload route, form handling, and JS drag/drop event listeners.

title: Sort accounts and wikis by recent activity on Explore page
Wikilink rewriting on page rename
description: Rename works but doesn't scan/rewrite [[old-path]] wikilinks. Silent link breakage = data integrity issue. Spec requires atomic rewrite of all referencing pages.
close_reason: Implemented rename/move wikilink rewriting and atomic repo updates in PATCH page handling.

title: TOC anchor scroll offset: sticky nav covers heading target
description: when clicking a TOC link, the page scrolls to the heading but the sticky top nav (56px) covers it. need scroll-padding-top or scroll-margin-top on heading elements so the anchor target appears below the nav bar.

title: Connection system: cross-referencing patterns that prevent noise
description:
## Context

Cross-references are the whole point of a wiki over flat notes. But random/spurious connections make it worse than no connections. This issue defines a connection system that maximizes signal and minimizes noise.

Tested on a 115-page wiki — the patterns below emerged from what worked and what didn't.

## The problem

Agents generating wiki pages tend to either:
- Add zero connections (each page is an island)
- Add random connections based on keyword overlap ("both mention 'learning'" → spurious link)

Both are bad. We need inline wikilinks woven naturally into prose — like Wikipedia, not like a "see also" appendix.

## How connections work: inline wikilinks in prose

Links are woven into the text naturally as the page is written:

> "Harrison worked at [[orbit-internship|Orbit]] where [[mark-khrapko|Mark Khrapko]] mentored him on [[hiring-and-teams|hiring philosophy]] and he developed the [[cnn-spectrogram-analysis|CNN spectrogram classifier]]"

No dedicated "connections" section. If a relationship matters, it belongs in the prose. If it's not worth mentioning in prose, it's not a real connection. Backlinks (auto-generated by renderer) handle the reverse direction.

### Rules for inline linking

- **Link on first mention only** — don't link the same page multiple times in one section
- **Use display text** — `[[slug|Natural Text]]` so prose reads naturally
- **Don't over-link** — link when it adds navigational value, not every noun
- **Don't self-link** — a page never links to itself
- **Conservative matching** — better to miss a link than create a wrong one. "Mark" alone is ambiguous; "Mark Khrapko" is not.

Agents creating wiki pages should include inline links AS THEY WRITE, not as a separate pass. The schema/CLAUDE.md should specify this behavior.

## Cross-section connection taxonomy

Agents should specifically look for these high-value connection types:
- **person ↔ topic**: conversation pages link to topics discussed with that person
- **course ↔ topic**: academic material links to personal exploration of same concepts
- **topic ↔ topic**: thematic overlap between different knowledge areas
- **experience ↔ reflection**: what happened links to what was learned from it

Cross-section connections are almost always more valuable than within-section.

## Quality rules (anti-spam)

**MUST:**
- Pass the test: "would a reader learn something by following this link?"
- Reference specific shared content, not just shared keywords

**SHOULD:**
- Every page should have at least 2 outbound inline links (prevents orphans)
- Inline links are uncapped but governed by "first mention, don't over-link"

**MUST NOT:**
- Link based solely on keyword overlap without semantic relationship
- Create a dedicated "connections" or "see also" section — if it's not in the prose, it's not a connection
- Link every page in the same category to each other

## Lint-based link discovery

Automated agent pass for existing wikis that lack inline links:
1. Build alias map: page slug → matching text patterns (full name, course code, key phrase)
2. Scan prose for mentions of other pages
3. Replace first occurrence with `[[slug|matched text]]`
4. Conservative: skip ambiguous matches
5. Flag pages with 0-1 outbound links for review

## Renderer support

- **Inline links**: render `[[slug|Display Text]]` as clickable links showing only display text
- **Backlinks panel**: auto-generated list of pages that link TO this page — this replaces any need for a "connections" section
- Optional: mini knowledge graph showing 1-hop connections from current page

title: Add people browsing and folder creation UX
description: Design and implement a way to browse users on the system, improve person/profile discoverability relative to wiki browsing, review sidebar universality, and add a simple UI affordance for creating folders. Update the spec and ship the first pass in product.
acceptance_criteria: Explore or adjacent navigation exposes people browsing; user profile clearly distinguishes personal wiki and other wikis; folder creation flow exists in web UI; spec is updated; e2e coverage added.
notes: Implement people browsing on Explore, strengthen profile as person page, add first-pass folder creation UI, update spec, and add e2e coverage.
close_reason: Implemented people browsing on /explore and /people, strengthened /@username as the canonical person page with personal wiki first, added folder creation UI via index.md scaffolding, updated the spec, and added e2e coverage.

title: v3 milestone
description: Advanced features: realtime collab, org/multi-tenant, encryption, moderation tooling.

title: Paste-unstructured-text → LLM generates wiki
description: Server-hosted coding agent with platform key. Leaning headless Claude Code over raw Anthropic tool-use. Needs agent orchestration layer.

title: Link-share token expiry
description: v1 link tokens are permanent. Add expiry syntax or Postgres-side override.

title: D3 force-graph render of wikilinks (site-wide)
description: Cross-wiki graph visualization. Extends the per-wiki v1.5 graph to full platform view.

title: git-crypt escape hatch for encrypted wikis
description: Encrypted personal wikis pattern from jacobcole.net/labs.

title: Multi-human-one-agent session semantics
description: Multiple humans collaborating through a single agent session.

title: Wikipedia-grade moderation tooling
description: Edit filters, trust levels, auto-revert bots. Full moderation suite.

title: Org / multi-tenant surface
description: Anthropic intranet target. Principal table shape is ready. Multi-tenancy / org support.

title: Signed-in mode, link-share mode ACL
description: Deferred ACL modes: signed-in, link-share, link:token:role grants, group:name:role grants.

title: URL / repo connect (import from public git repos)
description: git clone --bare + walk tree + index into Postgres. Plumbing exists in git_sync.py. Need UI form and background worker.

title: HTML / Google Sites importer
description: Import existing HTML sites/Google Sites into wiki format.

title: Inline diff UI for suggested edits
description: Fork is v1; suggest-edits-back-upstream PR-like mechanic (diff format, list UI, accept/reject, cherry-pick). No current user demand yet.

title: Friends-list / group creation UI
description: Grant syntax is v1, the management UI is v2+. Group CRUD, member management, use in ACL grants.

title: Real-time collaborative editing
description: CRDT/OT. Massive undertaking. Async-first, not biased toward realtime.

title: Server-hosted cloud agent
description: Talk-to-an-agent-we-host via web UI, per-user folder sandbox via UNIX permissions.

title: Featured curation admin surface
description: v1 uses most-starred automatically. Admin override for manual curation.

title: SFTP upload path
description: Session-batched commits, SSH key endpoint, sshfs/rsync compatible. Needs SSH server daemon.

title: Import curated ListHub content to WikiHub
description: Import select ListHub lists as wikis on WikiHub. Good candidates: Body Masters, Autodidacts, Mental State Hacks, Startup Ideas, Bay Area Activities, Supplements. Put on @jacobcole account. Feature best ones in @wikihub community index.
close_reason: Imported 7 ListHub items to @jacobcole/curated-lists. Created Featured Wikis page in @wikihub community wiki.

title: Wiki index pages rendered like Quartz — folder overview with page previews
description:
When you visit a wiki root or folder, the index page could show previews/excerpts of child pages — similar to how Quartz renders folder pages with cards showing page titles, excerpts, and metadata.

Currently wiki index pages are either a rendered index.md or a plain file listing. A Quartz-style index would automatically generate a richer overview with:
- Page title + first paragraph excerpt
- Last updated date
- Tags if present
- Maybe a card layout

This could be opt-in via frontmatter (layout: quartz-index) or the default when no index.md exists.

title: Review and triage spec/code audit findings (2026-04-10)
description: Full spec/code audit conducted 2026-04-10 by parallel Claude + Codex review. Found 44 issues across 4 severity tiers. Spec contradictions (category 1) already fixed in-session. Remaining: 4 critical code bugs, 10 high-priority gaps, ~20 medium missing features, ~10 low nits. Full findings in docs/audit-2026-04-10.md. This ticket: review the audit, create individual tickets for items worth tracking separately, and prioritize the critical 4 for immediate fix.
notes: Implementing the 2026-04-10 audit list end-to-end, starting with critical items and verifying with tests after each logical batch.
close_reason: Implemented the 2026-04-10 audit list and updated the checklist in docs/audit-2026-04-10.md.

title: draggable resize handle between content and right sidebar not implemented
description: the border between the article content and the right sidebar panel is not draggable. user wants to be able to resize the split. store width preference in localStorage. this was filed before as wikihub-9gm but confirming it's still needed.

title: import pipeline for Obsidian vaults and markdown repos
description: add Obsidian vault and plain markdown repo import. discover and convert [[wikilinks]] (already supported in renderer), preserve folder structure. extend existing zip upload flow. inspired by gbrain's discovery + import pipeline.

title: Add /tickets page — public beads ticket viewer
description: Add a web page (linked from footer) that renders the beads issue tracker as a public ticket board. Shows open issues, priorities, status. Could be a simple server-rendered page that reads .beads/issues.jsonl. Transparency for users about what's being worked on.
close_reason: Live at /roadmap — fetches issues.jsonl from GitHub, filterable tree with detail panel

title: Graph view: unbounded zoomable canvas instead of fixed bounding box
description: The graph view is too cramped with many notes. Replace the fixed bounding box with an unbounded canvas inside a clearly defined border-radius rectangle. When the mouse is inside the graph area and scrolling, zoom in/out smoothly into an unbounded inner graph space. Nodes remain clickable. This should make large wikis much more navigable — the current approach tries to fit everything into a tiny box which gets chaotic with many notes. Alternative considered: just making the sidebar wider, but unbounded zoom is the better UX.

title: No way to name/rename a page when creating from web UI
description: When creating a new page from the web UI, there's no clear way to set the page title/name. The page path becomes the title but there's no rename functionality. Need: title field on create form, rename option on existing pages (both web UI and keeping the existing API PATCH endpoint working).

title: recently updated feed on wiki homepage and profiles
description: show last N modified pages on wiki homepage and user profiles. data already available from git history. render as a 'recently updated' section like Farzapedia's homepage sidebar.

title: Polish people cards with visibility badges and richer profile metadata
description: Follow up on the first-pass people browsing feature by adding spec-consistent visibility badges on explore/profile cards, richer personal-wiki metadata on person cards, and finalizing mobile polish for the person-page sidebar.

title: Add one-click magic-link sign-in for agents
description: Let an authenticated agent generate a short-lived one-click sign-in link for the account owner, so the browser session can be established without typing credentials or pasting API keys. Update the spec and add e2e coverage.
acceptance_criteria: API endpoint returns a one-click login URL for the authenticated account; visiting the link logs the user into a browser session and invalidates the token; link expires; spec updated; e2e coverage added.
notes: Implement short-lived single-use magic login links for agent-generated browser sign-in, update spec, and add e2e coverage.
close_reason: Implemented short-lived single-use magic sign-in links via API and browser route, documented the agent flow, updated the spec, and added e2e coverage.

title: article type system with per-type rendering (person, book, place)
description: add a type: frontmatter key (person, book, place, artifact, assessment) with per-type rendering. person gets infobox, book gets cover, place gets map link. builds on infobox feature. inspired by Farzapedia's category system.

title: Editor preview tab shows plaintext instead of rendered markdown
description: The Preview tab in the editor (editor.html) shows raw markdown text via textContent, not rendered output. Needs either a client-side markdown renderer or an AJAX call to the server's render endpoint. Related to wikihub-18e (editor QA). This is the biggest UX gap in the editing experience.

title: Account dropdown menu disappears before clicking menu items
description: The account dropdown menu in the nav only works if you click the avatar icon. Hovering opens it but moving the mouse down to the menu items causes it to disappear before you can click them. Likely a gap between the trigger and the dropdown that breaks the :hover chain, or the mouseenter/mouseleave handlers are too aggressive. Needs a hover delay or bridge element.

title: Public edit (public-edit visibility) not working from web UI
description: Pages with public-edit visibility should be editable by any logged-in user, not just the owner. The web UI edit button may not appear or the route may reject non-owners even when the page allows public editing. Confirmed: API correctly rejects non-owner edits on non-public-edit pages. Need to verify the public-edit flow works end-to-end.

title: private wikis leak page existence and index content to unauthenticated users
description:
Two related access control issues with private wikis:

1. **Index page visible on private wikis** — if a wiki is fully private, visiting the wiki index still renders the page. It should deny access entirely (redirect to login or show a 'no access' message).

2. **Direct links to private pages show empty page shell** — if you have a direct link to a private page (e.g. from a search engine or shared URL), the route renders the page template with no content instead of showing a clear 'no access' or 'this page is private' message. This leaks that the page exists and looks like a bug to the user.

Expected behavior:
- Private wiki index: 403/404 or redirect, not a rendered page
- Direct link to private page: clear 'no access' message, not an empty page shell

title: Simplify account trigger to avatar-only in top nav
description: Remove the inline @username label from the authenticated account trigger in the top-right nav while keeping the username visible in the dropdown summary.
acceptance_criteria: Top nav account trigger shows avatar affordance without inline username text; dropdown still shows display name and @username.
close_reason: Removed inline username from top-nav account trigger, kept identity visible in dropdown, verified with full e2e suite, and pushed.

title: Profile vs personal wiki: clicking a person's name should go to /@username, not /@username/username
description:
On the Explore page, wiki cards for personal wikis (where slug == username) link to /@username/username (the wiki root), not /@username (the profile). This is confusing because:

1. The profile page IS the personal wiki rendered — they show the same content
2. But the profile has the user header, wiki sidebar with project wikis, and 'Wikis' section
3. The wiki root is just the wiki content without the profile chrome

For personal wikis, links should go to /@username (the profile) everywhere:
- Explore page wiki cards
- Search results
- Any place that shows a personal wiki as a card/link

For project wikis (slug != username), links should go to /@username/slug as normal.

The deeper question: should there even be a difference between /@username and /@username/username? Currently the profile page renders the personal wiki's index.md as its main content. Maybe /@username/username should just redirect to /@username. That would make the personal wiki's web identity always be the profile URL, while git clone still uses /@username/username.git.
close_reason: Personal wiki links go to profile. /@user/user redirects 302 to /@user.

title: v2 milestone
description: Core platform features: abuse protection, social features, content import, polish.

title: IP rate limit on writes (10/min/IP)
description: Anti-abuse priority #1. flask-limiter already referenced in app/__init__.py. Decorator config on write endpoints. Infra-level, not moderation.

title: Comments on pages and wikis
description: ACL vocabulary already reserves 'comment' role. Need Comment model, API endpoints, UI component. Social feature that drives engagement.

title: Omni-convert upload (PDF/DOCX → markdown)
description: Pandoc handles DOCX→MD well. PDF is messier (pdftotext or LLM). Could ship DOCX-only first as quick win — zip upload infra already exists.

title: Landing page live background
description: Featured/popular wiki cards slowly drifting/orbiting behind hero section. Pure CSS animation. Needs enough public wikis to look good — ship after dogfood migrations populate platform.

title: Panic button: disable anonymous writes per wiki
description: Anti-abuse priority #2. Toggle to instantly disable anonymous writes on a wiki. Quick emergency response for spam/abuse.

title: Revert last N anonymous edits button
description: Anti-abuse priority #3. Web UI button to bulk-revert recent anonymous edits. Recovery path after spam attacks.

title: External wiki references/aliases in sidebar — cross-wiki navigation
description:
Allow wikis to reference or alias other wikis (even from different users) in their sidebar navigation. Use case: the @wikihub community wiki could have a 'Featured Wikis' folder in its sidebar that lists @jacobcole/rsi-wiki, @jacobcole/admitsphere, etc. as if they were local pages.

This is conceptually like symlinks or bookmarks in a filesystem — you don't own the content, but you can navigate to it from your wiki's sidebar.

Possible implementations:
- A .wikihub/references file that lists external wiki paths to include in sidebar
- A special frontmatter field like 'sidebar_refs' on index.md
- A folder-like virtual entry that links to another wiki

This would make the community index wiki much more useful as a navigational hub — not just a page with links, but actual sidebar entries that feel like part of the wiki.

Inspired by how Quartz and other static site generators handle cross-references.

title: MCP server endpoint (/mcp)
description: Discovery endpoints exist (.well-known/mcp/server-card.json, /.well-known/mcp) but the actual /mcp endpoint serving MCP protocol is missing. Tools: whoami, search, read_page, list_pages, create_page, update_page, append_section, delete_page, set_visibility, share, create_wiki, fork_wiki, commit_log. This is the whole thesis — wikihub is GitHub for LLM wikis.
close_reason: Implemented /mcp with tool listing/call proxy behavior and wired discovery manifests.

title: Add subtle clone/download buttons to wiki pages
description: Add small, non-obtrusive clone URL and ZIP download buttons to wiki pages. Should be subtle — maybe in the breadcrumb area or as small icons. Show git clone URL (copyable) and ZIP download link. Already have /@user/wiki.zip endpoint and git clone works.
close_reason: Added subtle download (ZIP) and clone (copy URL) icons to reader and folder views

title: Populate @wikihub official wiki with starter content
close_reason: Created 5 pages: index, Getting Started, Agent Integration, API Reference, Examples

title: schema.md templates at wiki creation (freeform, structured, research)
description: rescoped: offer 2-3 schema.md templates when creating a wiki instead of the empty placeholder. templates teach agents how to structure pages, use wikilinks, track sources, and maintain compiled truth + timeline format. the templates ARE the agent instructions — no platform validation needed. templates: (1) freeform — minimal, just explains frontmatter and wikilinks (2) structured — compiled truth + timeline, typed pages, source tracking, connection rules (3) research — academic focus with course/topic/conversation types. also update the scaffold_wiki function to accept a template choice.

title: account menu button broken — click does nothing
description: the top-right account button stopped working after switching from hover to click-to-toggle. likely the JS click handler or CSS selector broke during the hover removal. check base.html — the .open class toggle and click-outside-to-close may have been disrupted. test on prod: https://wikihub.globalbr.ai

title: wiki homepage with categories, stats, and featured article
description: render a rich wiki homepage from frontmatter tags instead of a flat folder listing. group pages by type/category tag, show article count and category count, featured article section, browse by category grid. inspired by Farzapedia's '417 articles across 41 categories' homepage.
